planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or grafted trees in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun after all frost danger has passed; yellow mombin is damaged below 30°F. Large stem cuttings (hardwood) root readily and are a common propagation method in tropical countries. The tree drops its leaves briefly in dry season and fruits shortly after.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun in frost-free conditions; yellow mombin (hog plum) is a large deciduous tropical tree of Central and South America and the Caribbean producing clusters of small, oval, golden-yellow fruits, 1–2 inches long, with a distinctive sweet-tart, highly aromatic tropical flavor that combines elements of mango, plum, and passionfruit with a resinous, citrus-like quality. The thick, waxy skin encloses a small amount of translucent yellow flesh surrounding a large seed. In the Caribbean, Central America, and tropical Brazil, the fruit is eaten fresh, made into juice, agua fresca, ice cream, wine, and vinegar — Spondias mombin juice is a major street beverage in countries like Colombia, Costa Rica, and Brazil. The tree is fast-growing, highly drought-tolerant once established, and tolerates a range of soil conditions from light sandy to heavy clay. It is distinct from the ambarella (S. dulcis) and red mombin/jocote (S. purpurea).
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun in frost-free to near-frost-free conditions; lucuma is damaged below 28–30°F but tolerates brief cool temperatures better than most tropical fruit trees. Grafted trees begin fruiting in 3–5 years versus 7–10 years for seedlings. Good drainage is essential — it does not tolerate waterlogged soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun in a frost-free location; lucuma is a Peruvian fruit tree native to the Andean valleys, where it has been cultivated since ancient times and was called the 'Gold of the Incas.' The large, oval fruits (3–4 inches) have a dry, starchy, bright orange-yellow flesh with an extraordinary flavor — intensely sweet with a rich, complex caramel-maple-sweet potato quality that is completely unlike any familiar fruit. The taste is so distinctive and rich that lucuma powder (dried, ground lucuma flesh) is used as a natural sweetener and flavoring for ice cream, smoothies, pastries, and confections in Peru, where lucuma ice cream outsells chocolate and vanilla in many surveys. The flavor is particularly popular as a low-glycemic sweetener alternative. Fresh lucuma is eaten locally in Peru and Chile but ships poorly, so it is rarely found fresh outside its home region. An exceptional specialty fruit tree for collectors in frost-free zones.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in moist, well-drained soil in spring or fall; date plum is adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions and is notably more cold-hardy than Asian persimmon. Female trees require a male pollinator for reliable fruit set. It is commonly used as a rootstock for grafting Diospyros kaki.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, adaptable soil in full sun to part shade; date plum is a medium to large tree native from the Caucasus and Mediterranean to eastern Asia, producing small (1/2–1 inch) yellow to blue-black persimmon fruits in October–November that resemble tiny plums and have a sweet, date-like flavor — hence 'date plum.' The fruit contains high tannin levels when unripe (astringent like American persimmon) but becomes sweet after frost or extended hanging on the tree. Historical records suggest the fruit may be the legendary 'lotus fruit' of Homer's Odyssey that made Odysseus's sailors forget their homes. The tree is highly ornamental with glossy, dark-green leaves that turn yellow and red in fall, interesting orange bark on mature specimens, and small, attractive cream-colored flowers. More cold-hardy than Asian persimmon and easier to establish in temperate gardens, it is used as a rootstock for kaki persimmons.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings easily in moist soil or water; bele grows as a perennial shrub in zones 9–11 and cuttings establish quickly. It thrives in hot, humid conditions and tolerates partial shade — often growing more productively in light shade in the hottest months, which reduces leaf toughening and extends the harvest season.

directions: Root cuttings and plant in fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; bele (aibika) is a large-leafed tropical relative of okra grown widely across Papua New Guinea, the Pacific Islands, and Southeast Asia as one of the most important leafy vegetables — the large, soft, mucilaginous leaves are harvested young and eaten as a cooked green vegetable, used to thicken soups and stews, or wrapped around other foods before cooking, exactly as collard or banana leaves are used. The mucilage thickens soups in the same way okra does. The large, showy yellow hibiscus flowers with dark centers are ornamental and also edible. Bele is heat-tolerant, productive, and thrive in Gulf Coast zone 9 summers when other leafy greens bolt and fail. It grows as a perennial shrub in frost-free conditions and can be harvested continuously. Rich in protein relative to most leafy vegetables.
sources: Pacific Community Plant Resources for Food and Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; wampee is sensitive to cold and is best protected from any frost. It is smaller and more compact than most tropical fruit trees, reaching 10–20 feet, and can be grown in large containers in cooler climates and moved indoors in winter.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun in frost-protected conditions; wampee (wampi) is a Chinese subtropical fruit tree in the citrus family (Rutaceae) producing large drooping clusters of small, round, yellowish-white to golden fruits about the size of a large grape, with a thin, slightly oily skin and aromatic, translucent flesh that ranges from sweet to pleasantly acid-sweet, with a distinctive, complex, resinous-floral fragrance from essential oils in the skin. The flavor profile has notes of citrus, lychee, and a slight bitterness that makes it uniquely refreshing. Native to South China and widely grown in southern China, Vietnam, and Thailand, it has been cultivated for over 2,000 years and is a significant regional fruit crop in Guangdong. The fruits are eaten fresh, made into preserves and wine, and the essential oil from the rind is used in Chinese medicine and as a fragrance. It grows well in the humid subtropics of zones 9–11 and is more compact and manageable than many tropical fruit trees.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit
